item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The new image is a combination of: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:FullMoon2010.jpg relased under CC 3.0
and:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/243_Ida#/media/File:243_Ida_-_August_1993_(16366655925).jpg released under CC-BY-SA 2.0

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a series of edits by KiGavin was reverted, as they were more than simple grammar fixes (stylistic edits) and require author or staff permission prior to being made.

Editor, please communicate with the author before making these sorts of changes from now on. Author, if the editor had permission and the revert occurred in error, please state so.

item.title: Re: +300
item.content:encoded:
Effects have defects take a bow to the moon.

Brain wave, main wave, psycho got a high kick?

Congrats on the +300!

Effects have defects take a bow to the moon.

I have the biggest grin on my face right now. This was one of my hardest SCPs to write, and as far as I can tell, it was one of my first SCPs to be featured on Tv Tropes— specifically on the Nightmare Fuel page for the Foundation. Seeing this cross +300 makes me feel so giddy.

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SHIT. COGNITOHAZARD AND INFOHAZARD SCP'S SCARE ME.

+1

item.title: Some Thoughts
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I've got a lot to say about this SCP.

It's very well written has some potential, even if it is just scipped version of
Susan Beth Pfeffer's Life as We Knew it. But before I can upvote this, I need some explanations/modifications.

First, I've got a few questions, particularly concerning this article. First of all, does the object effect those aware of it, or only those who are refered to it with a hand jesture/pointer/etc.? This makes a big difference. Second of all, the volcanoes stuff doesn't make a lot of sense. See physics complaints above. Third of all, the way this is written, it's heavily implied that there is a memetic innoculation agent/counter-meme mental exercise. That way, the O5 Council could have acess to the information. It also would make some of the research done on this thing easier overall management by the Foundaiton plausible.

My heacannon regarding this article is that the meme is a cognitohazard, that causes the affected individual to perceive the enviorment as thought SCP-1812 did exist, and be affected as though this were reality. So what would be the appropriate way to treat/develope resistance to SCP-1812? Instead of insisting that the water doesn't exist, or that the light doesn't exist, convince them that SCP-1812 doesn't exist, because it doesn't. It's just another part of the cognitohazard.

Scentence: Downvote. For now at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or, it could be as simple as "writing gullible on the ceiling".
Someone walks up to him, points up in the sky, and jokingly says "Look! Another moon!" Then Patient Zero looks up and happens to be at the right place and time to see Luna 2 being pointed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's the original subgenius. The one who can naturally see the fnords.

That or he was just crazy enough to be able to see it naturally, without having to be "infected" with the particular variety of crazy that 1812 causes… And everything else from there is history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it depends if they've been clued in to water being imaginary, of course. Maybe it would be possible to tell that that anything that has water in and looks either clear or like muddied-up seawater is just a figment of their imagination, but drinks that look different are still real? They can at least be given juice, tea, tap water with some red dye in it, etc, to keep them hydrated then.

Plus, SCUBA equipment isn't something you screw into your face with a special tool and that emits air continually at a steadily declining rate until the tank is empty. The mouthpiece contains a clever bit of science (called a demand valve, or DV) that holds the airline closed until you actively inhale from it, then opens to admit a lungful, then closes again when you finish. The far end of its air hose is connected to a pair of devices (the main regulator, and the octopus) that pull the tank's actually far higher pressure (up to 300 bar, usually) down to just a little over the current environmental pressure, and in fact maintains that output level accross several different accessories, auto compensating for additional equipment and current depth.

No air should enter or exit such a system if the components are in good order and no-one is sucking on the main or backup DV at the time.

So you could just hang around chilling out more or less forever, with only the most forfeiiiiiooo [excess letters removed]
…wow what the fuck

Anyway, with the SCUBA kit being essentially "off" unless someone sucks on a DV, you could just wander around with it on if your back is in decent condition, and only need to be in your mouch and losing air from the tank if you perceive the water level as deep enough to spreven you rongr…

OK, either I'm having microsleeps at exacly the wrong time, or maybe they're actau===== /…. ok what… a serieas of one sized narror=t….
I'm sure I'm writing things here but then I find myself opening my eyes again and they're not as they were. Help.

Please don't make long lines of letters like that, it's extremely inconvenient for people browsing the site using Recent Posts, as well as anyone attempting to read this page of the discussion thread on a mobile device. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the implication is that it is indeed fictional, and an anomalous meme, which has serious psychosomatic effects (including spontaneous human combustion - finally a reason for it?) arising from its cognitohazardous effect.

+ show block
– hide block

Including, for example, perceiving that you are entering a body of water, which will stop you from breathing, but finding to your utter terror that *it provides no bouyancy whatsoever*. Thus you cannot do anything to keep your head above it. Rather like an ocean of CO2 or similar.

The horizontal movement difficulties are likely more memetic - if you've ever tried to walk, rather than swim around in deepish water, particularly tidal sea water with strong waves, you'll know that it's pretty difficult even though the density is only slightly greater than 1.0 g/ml, due to the drag effects and its own momentum. Like a strong wind, but without needing to move fast to cause serious difficulty. And if tides have become massively intensified, with a range of many tens of metres where previously they might have only gone up and down by 2 or 3 metres instead, that'll create one mother of a riptide. People can already easily be pulled out to sea, and sucked underwater, with just one much weaker moon moving things around.

(plus rivers can become impossible to wade or even drive across without being knocked over or washed away, even at points where they're only a few inches deep, when their flow rate gets much beyond walking pace… moving water is a forceful thing)

And then when you consider they're attempting to wade and, if the level gets higher, swim through entirely imaginary water that therefore doesn't provide any kind of significant propulsive force in response to arm strokes or leg kicks, or sculling of the surface… but are still affected by what the water itself does… that's a recipe for becoming totally caught up in the current and unable to make headway.

The bit about it seeming to be much denser is entirely superfluous in light of that, really. It doesn't NEED to be denser, and in fact any substance with the stated density, on earth at STP, would either be a solid, or a liquid so viscous and slow flowing that it would make the pitch drop experiment seem rapid, and therefore may as well be solid when measured on a human timescale.

For the sake and sanity of our mobile readers, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The results of attempting to "cure" the meme at the end really made it.

Also I hated that it was circled in such an obvious photo until I read the description. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree on this, especially since it said its first discovery was made by a man, who did not leave any record of the finding, nor was there any information discovered other then how he died. I agree with you on this. But overall an interesting and frightening idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed the post. The aspect that persons who have become aware of 1812 believe, and in fact are surrounded by a water type substance that no one else can perceive, in and of itself is terrifying. Not to mention the repercussions of convincing the person who is aware that the water is not real, has a detrimental effect on the person involved, is frightening as well. Really enjoyed this one for sure. Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent MacLeod asked something similar, but can the infected now breathe "underwater" at low tide?
Is their movement hindered? You couldn't just drop them off with SCUBA to test it, because, to them, it would be at altitude. Just drop an object to be recovered a few hundred meters off the normal low-tide mark and ask them to recover it. Observe movement, respiration, and ability to obtain the object. Later, you could see if they could ride a bike "underwater" or operate a car.
If that checks out, infected class Ds could be a boon for underwater operations (although they DO require evac before the tide comes back in…).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: big inconsistancy, i might be wrong but it states that it can only be discovered by someone who knows about it telling you its there, so how is it discovered in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Describing 670g/cm3 as "unusually high density" is an understatement. That's four times denser than the core of the sun. That's one and a half International Space Stations per cubic meter. There's no conventional material that this thing could be made of.

Blowing it up seems like a terrible idea — on the off-chance that it somehow exists, I wouldn't be surprised at all if it's not some space station containing some miniaturized fucking sun.

And on the off-chance that it's some basically harmless but superdense substance, suppose they wanted to blow half of it off and pull it away from the other half. The escape velocity of one half from the other is in the area of 60m/s. Moving 9x1016kg at 60m/s is about 5x1018kg*m/s. The relation between kinetic energy and momentum is K=p2/(2m), or about 1.3x1020J, or about 130,000 PetaJoules. Wolfram Alpha puts this on the order of the energy released by the 2004 Indian Earthquake, and Wikipedia puts it at 619 Tsar Bombas, or 60 times the amount of all nuclear testing done ever. I can't find good numbers on how this compares to the combined nuclear arsenals of the United States and Russia, but I can't imagine that it's favorable.

My point is that nuking it will either unleash fuckin armageddon or do absolute jack shit.

item.title: Misleading name?
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to point out that "extralunar" means "outside the moon", not literally "another moon". I think you might want to change the wording to avoid being mistaken for that meaning, because I kept looking for something about Luna 2 that made it specifically "extralunar". It certainly doesn't orbit outside the moon's orbit, or we wouldn't be able to see it with the naked ee or experience its tides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test idea: expose someone to the meme, put them in an SCBA (SCUBA, but without the Underwater), tell them it IS designed for underwater use, put them somewhere that would normally be deadly, and see what happens. If the liquid that affected individuals see is real, they'll start to drown. If it isn't, they'll be fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked this one since I first read it, but for the more physics inclined, would a nuclear bombardment do anything to Luna 2 other than some new, radioactive craters?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if it would be possible to stabilize the effects if enough people could see it…

Subject Zero was actually marooned on the rooftop, and could actually physically interact with the water, right? Perhaps his escape plan failed, not simply because the water didn't exist, but because the boat wasn't under 1812's effects due to being an inanimate object (and simply fell off the roof).

Here's the question: did the guy fall with the boat, survive the impact, and subsequently drown due to a mind-over-matter effect? Or did the guy land in the water, gradually sink to ground level, and actually drown?

In other words, how "there" is the water?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this thing terrifying. All it would take for this thing to spread worldwide is one person noticing it and pointing it out to another, who in turn would point it out to another, etc. etc.

Wait what.

Well, this came right the crap out of nowhere! Thanks, guys. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like the last addendum, but I like the rest of it rather a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if you use amnestics on someone affected by Luna 2? If you make them forget the object exists, are they still able to perceive its effects?

+1 overall; it's a neat concept and I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And if it were real, the memetic hazard would cause hallucinations once you looked at it because you'd know of its existence. But what if it WERE real, and believing it isn't even after you see it doesn't cause any effects? I'd say that should be added to the article, but in-universe, if this document were leaked to the public, saying "Believing it isn't real in the first place cancels out the effects" would probably make the public more easily believe it's real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this is disturbing…
If this was real, coastal cities would be flooded wastelands and areas near the center of continents would be prime real estate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the black moon Luna 2 howl?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. You just got this article an upvote for your headcanon now being stuck in mine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I think that this is a coexistant-reality situation, layering based. When people don't know that LUNA 2 exists, they are on layer A. When someone realizes it exists, Layer B is unhidden, but it doesn't often overlap Layer A. The victim is on both layers A (our standard life) and B (the "flood life"), and can percieve things from both, but everything under the overlap is permanantly hidden for the victim, so if you suddenly hide Layer B (which overlaps Layer A in areas of Water and Solar Light), the corresponding parts of Layer A are hidden to the "victim" as well, but because the victim is primarily in Layer A, they are still effected by the hidden sections.

Therefore, LUNA 2 does exist, but not on this layer of reality. At least, that's how my headcanon goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh, even then there are too many secondary effects that COULDN'T be ignored. NYC = totally underwater city = all that brickwork and structural steel constantly exposed to seawater = massive amounts of corrosion/erosion compared to "dry" cities. Even if we could ignore the structural damage, are we supposed to believe that people are living in buildings that have already fallen down? Again, Occam's Razor says that this thing is adding perceptions rather than removing filters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's effect is that as long as people don't know it exists, they don't perceive it's effects.

On other words, New York is really an underwater city. People just don't realize it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either a handful of people have an infectious delusion or all the rest of humanity, extending back past the beggining of recorded history, are the deluded ones. Add in the fact that the behavior of inanimate objects (like Mr. Montag's improvised boat) support the single-moon hypothesis, and it's pretty clear which alternative is more likely.

But the Foundation assumes that Luna 2 exists? This doesn't call for Occam's Razor so much as Occam's Chainsaw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me until the last addendum. Not too happy about the hypoxia bit.

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So if I understand it right, this is a secondary moon, with effects taking effect (as weird as it might sound) only for those who are aware of the moon. Okay.
First I thought it's going to be some kind of an armaggedon-esque meteorite (with the nuclear bombing and trying to prevent public knowledge), but the idea itself was quite nice. And when I saw the photo and the description, I had this nudge to look out of the window… hm.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the "██ meters higher than the norm at low tide" be "██ meters lower than the norm at low tide", if there is a larger gravitational pull meaning larger extremes or does it create the perception of an increased volume of water overall?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in the sandbox, like it here. The nyctophobia thing seems a little tacked on, but that's a nitpick. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I like it…

item.title: I May Have Misunderstood...
item.content:encoded: …But if it requires someone to point out Luna 2 for you to see it, how did the first guy spot it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the airplane thing bugged me a little, too. Again, I can see flying into an airport of a coastal city, because presumably they go "under" the high tide at some point during approach/landing. But just flying over an ocean wouldn't do it. There's no way that a tide, no matter how severe, would bring an ocean level high enough to match the altitude planes fly at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the thing is that that thing in Samothrace can't be objectively verified OR denied. This could be, just based on the science that we know, use and love. I mean, not just tides, but check out orbits of the Moon and the ISS. Both would be affected by a nearby gravity well like Luna 2, especially the ISS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Allow me to address this: I know the physics are wonky, and I should have had the calculations checked over. Basically, I just put in some random numbers and saw if they would stick. I forgot that density existed all together; I'm not exactly a physics major, in case it hasn't been made abundantly clear. x_x

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The science doesn't bother me; a lot of the stuff the Foundation catalogs defy standard scientific analysis, and the fact is that we know very, very little about Luna 2 (the article even says "we assume it exists" - even that isn't definite).

My only issue is the air travel thing… it seems superfluous and leads me to questions like "what if an affected individual flew into/out of a costal airport like Boston or NYC?" I don't think it's necessary; clearly if they're airborne they're above the perceived depth of the liquid, so it wouldn't affect them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actualy, that's the line that made me check the comments.
I got here specifically to ask if it shouldn't be 'memetic danger', at least.
And a little lower I think the word 'meme' is used again. Feels out of place when the concept is 'memetic', and is being used on an oficial document….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but your calculations are all wrong. An object in the orbit described would take about 90 minutes to circle the Earth, not 8/9 of a day. The tidal effect wouldn't be anywhere near as strong as you describe if the object was made of ordinary rock, but you could fix that by explaining that it seems to be made of some super-high-density substance.

The height of the tides induced on Earth will be proportional to the mass of the inducing body divided by the cube of its distance (from the centre of the Earth, not the surface). If the object induces tides about as strong as the Sun, which is about half as strong as the Moon (which is about as weak as it could be and still have anything like the described effects), its mass is 1.8e17 kg. With a diameter of 8 km, this gives it a density of 670 g/cm3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chiming in on this one. The phrase 'meme warning' just makes me thing some one is posting from 9gag

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fractions.
I hate them.
2 things feel wonky:
The volcano thing seems to take the effect too far.
And who says / why would anyone think it entered Earth's orbit 3.2 billion years ago?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps "MEMETIC HAZARD WARNING" or something along those lines would work better then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I pity the werewolves.
And maybe THIS explains why some people are afraid to go in the water!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realize the math is wonky, but in my defense: gravitational physics is HARD. And they're just assuming it exists because it's easier to write a document that way, and they don't want another situation like that thing in Samothrace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is pretty awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have the time to do the math, but I don't think that an asteroid of that size & distance would exert sufficient gravitational force to make the tides that much more extreme. Also, I'm curious as to why the Foundation assumes that Luna 2 exists, considering that the vast majority of humanity is able to move about "underwater" just fine. Occam's Razor would suggest that the Luna 2 phenomenon is adding false perceptions to observers rather than removing any.

I'm just not sure what to think about this one. No vote right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly bizarre. I rather like the memetic aspect, in particular the reveal about how, when I looked at the photo, I could only see Luna-2 in the image because it was circled.

However I admit I am confused as to whether persons perceiving the change in sea level are actually affecting the sea level for everyone else. I'm assuming not, which means this thing is Keter because of its potential to massively change life on Earth if enough people know about it to point it out to everyone else. Effectively, Luna-2 messes things up just by existing in the minds of enough people, so containment hinges on it not existing. Yea, I like that.

It's a bit long, though. The impeded movement/swimming through air is a bit overboard, especially the implication that the medium is thicker than water. If it's mostly tides that are altered, wouldn't it just be sea water? I like the drowning and the bit about the boat in the recovery is great, but I think the article could stand a bit of trimming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found fault with that too: not all memes are hazardous. All information contains memes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: MEME WARNING

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very strange, but oddly appealing. I like. Upvote get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's/its always something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Several its/it's errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here goes.

Special thanks to anqxyr for several good ideas, and to everyone in chat who looked it over.
